data.title: CrowdStrike Has More Potential as AI Beneficiary — Market Talk
data.description: Wall Street is underestimating CrowdStrike's potential as a "second/third derivative beneficiary of the AI Revolution," say Wedbush analysts, who maintain their $600 price target. Shares could hit $700 over the next year in a bull case scenario, with AI fueling more enterprise cybersecurity spending. "We believe increased market and mind share is happening for CrowdStrike among new and existing customers," the analysts say. "CRWD has taken key strategic steps to integrate AI across its portfolio to capitalize on elevated demand for advanced cyber solutions to protect IT and AI infrastructure across the tech stack." Shares rise 0.6% to $480. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

data.title: Meet the BofA Investment Banker Vying to Represent Haiti in the Winter Paralympics — WSJ
data.description:
Alexander Saeedy | Photography by Nicolas Blandin for WSJ

Most investment bankers barely have time for life outside of work. Then there is Ralf Etienne, a banking associate with one leg, who is training to compete in the Winter Paralympics for his home country, Haiti.

Etienne, 36 years old, works for Bank of America in London. He spends most of his waking days in Excel and PowerPoint, building financial models and helping pitch deals to big companies and the bank's private-equity clients.

But on the weekends, he uses the limited free time he has to train with the Swiss Paralympic ski team in the Alps.

"It's a constant rotation: office, airport, mountain, back to the desk," Etienne said during an interview. "There's no comfort zone."

He has under three months to compete in international tournaments and qualify for the Winter Paralympic Games' three-track ski races, meant for athletes who compete on one leg. Haiti has already designated him as the Caribbean country's first-ever representative at the Games, which will take place this March in Italy.

If he makes it, Etienne will be one of just a few Paralympians who also work on Wall Street, where hard-charging bosses and long hours are the norm.

"Sometimes I'm leaving the office at 2 a.m. because I have work I need to finish before I get on a 6 a.m. flight to get to Switzerland," he said.

Etienne moved to London this year after leaving the U.S., where he had lived for years on a temporary work visa. The Department of Homeland Security started to put restrictions on Haitians' immigration rights in March, and Etienne worried he wouldn't be allowed to re-enter the country after traveling abroad for ski competitions.

The bank agreed to move him to London, not too far from the Alps. The hurried relocation has him behind schedule on training, but it has given him at least some peace of mind.

"The immigration process doesn't look at who I am or what I bring to the table. It just sees me as a case number," he said. "So it came down to a choice: Stay in a place that couldn't decide if it wanted me, or bet on myself, move to Europe and keep the momentum alive."

Etienne grew up outside of Port-au-Prince, the capital of Haiti. When he was 20 years old, he lost his leg during an earthquake in 2010 that killed more than 100,000 people. He was on the top floor of a four-story building in the capital that day, and both of his legs were crushed by the collapsing structure.

"I was hanging upside down for eight hours," he said. "They had to carve my flesh out of the building to get me out. Then they pushed me on a wheelbarrow to my hometown."

In the hospital, Etienne met Gregory Adamson, an American orthopedic surgeon who had traveled to Haiti to help the injured. Adamson told him to come to the U.S., where he would help Etienne get a prosthetic leg.

Etienne eventually stayed with Adamson and his family for three months in Illinois while undergoing the procedure, and they encouraged him to move to the U.S. for college. He enrolled as an undergraduate student at Bergen Community College in Paramus, N.J., in 2011, but after depleting his savings and sleeping on friends' couches for months, he felt he needed to make a change.

"I was running out of resources and realized if I don't find a better opportunity, I'm gonna have to emigrate back to Haiti," he said.

Etienne hopped on a bus and traveled from college to college to pitch himself and get a scholarship. That led him to Anderson University, a Christian university about 45 miles northeast of Indianapolis. Etienne graduated and then moved back to Haiti to work with charitable organizations. Four years later, he enrolled in business school at the University of North Carolina.

During the last year of his M.B.A. program, he caught the ski bug.

Etienne joined a group of fellow students on a ski trip to Lake Tahoe in the Sierra Nevada, but he didn't get to ski. At the resort, the instructors told him he required special lessons that had to be booked far in advance.

He vowed to find a way to make it down the slopes — except his banking career was about to start.

Etienne got a job in New York as an associate at Bank of America in 2022. He was recruited out of business school and started working in the private-equity group. When he could arrange a few days off, he would train with instructors in adaptive skiing around the U.S.

It wasn't until earlier this year that he realized he could even qualify for the Paralympics.

On a trip to Park City, Utah, Etienne met Monte Meier, a decorated paralympic skier. Meier said he was impressed by Etienne's abilities and told him he seemed to have what it takes to compete in the Games, which were coming up in about a year's time.

"He had such a strong 'I can' belief in himself that was kind of contagious," Meier said.

In the spring, Etienne took most of his paid time off and started training with a Paralympic skiing team that Meier coached. In April, he successfully completed his first competitive race in Winter Park, Colo., which made him eligible to compete in the Winter Paralympics. Haiti nominated him as the country's representative soon after.

At the same time, the Trump administration started to crack down on immigration rights for people from a number of countries, including Haiti. Companies recommended that foreigners on work visas not leave the U.S. unless absolutely necessary. Etienne worked with Bank of America to find a new job outside of the country.

"Suddenly I was spending months dealing with paperwork, relocating across continents, rebuilding my entire life from scratch," he said. "It meant losing time on the snow."

He says it was the right call. In December, the Department of Homeland Security said it would stop processing green-card applications for Haitians, which leaves people like Etienne without a path to citizenship.

Now, he is settled in his new job in London. His team at the bank is allowed to work remotely for the last two weeks of the year, which he is using to be in Switzerland to train. He plans to compete in 10 more races in Europe before the Paralympics open in March.

Etienne said that it hasn't been easy balancing his high-pressure job and training for the Paralympics. But he says he has learned how to adapt to two demanding life objectives.

"The bank didn't hire me to be a Paralympian," he said. "It actually makes me a better banker."

Write to Alexander Saeedy at alexander.saeedy@wsj.com

data.title: Tesla Stock Drops. Can It Reach $500 by 2026? — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Nate Wolf

Shares of Tesla dropped Friday, extending the stock's losing streak to three sessions to end the holiday-shortened week.

Safety concerns have dominated the conversation around Tesla stock in recent days. On Tuesday, the U.S. auto safety regulator opened an investigation into the mechanical door release on the 2022 Tesla Model 3. A petition alleged the door release was difficult to identify and use in an emergency.

The company is also nearing its self-imposed target to launch fully driverless robo-taxis, with no safety monitors, by the end of 2025. The prospect of a potentially lucrative robo-taxi business has helped Tesla stock overcome political opposition to electric vehicles this year.

Tesla shares, which are up 18% in 2025, fell 1.4% to $478.78 on Friday. The stock will need a strong run to finish the year above $500 a share after it hit an intraday high of $498.83 on Monday.

The car maker hasn't often benefited from a Santa Claus rally — the bump that U.S. stocks historically get in the last five trading days of December and first two sessions of January.

Tesla stock has declined in nine of 15 Santa Claus-rally periods as a publicly traded company, with a median drop of 2.7%, according to Dow Jones Market Data. Shares have dropped in three straight rally periods, including a 4.7% decline last year.

The S&P 500 has fallen in just four of the last 15 rally periods, per Dow Jones Market Data.

Write to Nate Wolf at nate.wolf@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: More rain expected in drenched California before drier weekend
data.description:
By Rich McKay

Storm-hit Southern California was at risk on Friday of more floods hampering millions of motorists traveling after Christmas, but the National Weather Service predicts a drier weekend.

The holiday deluge that started in earnest on Christmas Eve was spawned by the region's latest atmospheric storm, a vast airborne current of dense moisture siphoned from the Pacific, that swept inland over the greater Los Angeles area.

It dumped 6 inches of rain in the Los Angeles area with up to 18 inches of rain in the mountains, washing out some roads, and spurred evacuations and some shelter-in-place orders.

An additional 1-to-3 inches of rain is expected on Friday, said Tom Kines, a senior meteorologist with AccuWeather, a commercial forecasting company.

"Our overall picture is that there's just one more day of this mess, mostly across Southern California, specifically in the LA area," Kines said on Friday. "We still have some issues today with bouts of heavy rain, but this weekend is mainly dry, thankfully."

More than 14.5 million Californians were expected to travel by car over the Christmas holiday, according to AAA. The coming drier weather should make traveling easier, after days of slick or flooded roads, forecasters said.

The atmospheric river that brought the trouble will wind down through Friday across California with lingering heavy rainfall, heavy mountain snow, and gusty winds.

Many of the evacuation warnings issued in Los Angeles and San Bernardino counties were lifted on Christmas Day. But the orders remain on Friday in the hard-hit town of Wrightwood, a rural community with a population of about 5,000 in the San Gabriel Mountains on the border between Los Angeles and San Bernardino counties.

Aerial video footage posted online on Christmas Eve by the fire department showed rivers of mud coursing through inundated cabin neighborhoods, and mud-covered cars and homes.

Videos posted online on Thursday showed some residents scrambling over washed-out roads, picking through rubble on Christmas Day as streams of water still flowed over mounds of mud and into gullies that were once streets.

data.title: Wedbush Doubles Down on Crowdstrike as Cyber Meets AI Play Into 2026
data.description:
CrowdStrike Holdings, Inc. is shaping up as one of Wedbush's top tech picks heading into 2026, as the firm highlighted the company as a leading way to play the convergence of cybersecurity and artificial intelligence. Wedbush Securities reiterated its Outperform rating and $600 price target, calling CrowdStrike a core holding on its IVES AI 30 list.

Analysts led by Dan Ives said they are seeing deal momentum broaden across both new and existing customers, supported by expanding adoption of CrowdStrike's platform. Strength has been particularly notable in Cloud, Identity, LogScale, and Data Protection, while Charlotte AI has shown rising deal flow that appears to be accelerating into the January quarter.

Wedbush noted that enterprises are still in the early innings of AI-driven cybersecurity spending, with module add-ons increasing across CrowdStrike's core verticals. The firm expects Net New ARR growth to reaccelerate in 2026 and said a bull case could see shares reach $700 over the next year.

data.title: NYMEX Overview: Petroleum Futures Moving Lower in Morning Trade — OPIS
data.description:
Petroleum futures prices were falling at midday Friday, pushed lower in light post-holiday trade as efforts to broker an end to the Russia-Ukraine war remain in the news.

Oil prices were down by about $1/bbl at 11:30 a.m. ET, with February West Texas Intermediate crude off by $1.01 to $57.34/bbl and March prices down 99cts to $57.14/bbl. Both February and March Brent crude were down by $1 to $61.24 and $60.80/bbl, respectively.

With the end of the month approaching, most activity in refined products was focused on February contracts.

February RBOB prices were 2.98cts lower to $1.7243/gal while lightly traded January futures were down 3.16cts to $1.7155/gal. February ULSD was moving 1.98cts lower to $2.1339/gal while January futures fell by 2.3cts to $2.1346/gal.

Even with the morning's losses, most petroleum futures are on track to end the shortened holiday week with gains, though any increase in selling could send ULSD to a loss.

Prices are facing headwinds Friday as Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky said he plans to meet with President Trump on Sunday to discuss U.S. efforts to broker a peach between his country and Russia.

Reports indicate Zelensky seems to fell progress is being made on a U.S. proposal to end the fighting. A peace deal could lead to an easing of Western sanctions on Russian petroleum exports.

The three-day federal holiday around Christmas means that the Energy Information Administration delayed release of its weekly petroleum inventory and demand data, with data for the week ending Dec. 19 not being released until next week.

This content was created by Oil Price Information Service, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. OPIS is run independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

Reporting by Steve Cronin, scronin@opisnet.com

data.title: Profound Medical Slides on Upsized Private Placement
data.description:
By Katherine Hamilton

Shares of Profound Medical declined after the medical-device company said it planned to increase the size of its private placement.

The stock fell 2.5%, to $7.32, late Friday morning, and shares are down about 2.4% this year.

The Canadian company said Wednesday it would expand its private placement to an issuance of 921,428 shares at a price of $7 each.

Profound Medical first announced the private placement on Dec. 19, planning to offer up to 571,428 shares for $7 each.

It now expects gross proceeds to be up to about $6.5 million.

The purpose of increase is to accommodate participation by a long-term existing investor, Profound Medical said.

The company plans to use the proceeds to expand its sales and marketing, as well as for working capital, research and development and strategic transactions.

Write to Katherine Hamilton at katherine.hamilton@wsj.com

data.title: Nvidia Has Weak Defensive Rationale for Groq Deal — Market Talk
data.description: Nvidia's licensing agreement with Groq makes little sense from a technological perspective, but even the defensive rationale is difficult to see, says D.A. Davidson analyst Alex Platt. In the competitive landscape, Nvidia really only has to be concerned about Google and while Google's threat is growing, it's still not big enough to justify this kind of move. Nvidia could be seeing something in Groq's future chip generations but even so, "best case isn't a great case," Platt says. "We find it hard to believe there aren't better assets in the same market for NVIDIA to take a look at." (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

data.title: CrowdStrike Stock in Focus -- Wedbush Calls It 'AI Cybersecurity Leader' Ahead of 2026
data.description:
CrowdStrike Holdings is drawing investor attention as analysts highlight its role in the growing intersection of cybersecurity and artificial intelligence.

Wedbush kept an Outperform rating with a $600 price target, noting the stock remains a core pick on the IVES AI 30 list.

Analysts led by Dan Ives said CrowdStrike is seeing robust adoption across Cloud, Identity, and Logscale platforms, alongside stronger-than-expected traction in Data Protection and its Charlotte AI suite. These trends are supporting revenue growth in AI-linked cybersecurity solutions.

Revenue tied to cyber-AI products has risen above the year-ago period, with early indicators suggesting acceleration, Ives said. Increased AI-related threats are driving enterprise adoption of advanced cyber defenses, benefiting CrowdStrike's Falcon platform.

Wedbush analysts added that the market may underestimate CrowdStrike's potential as a secondary beneficiary of the AI revolution. They see CRWD shares possibly reaching $700 over the next year, supported by recurring revenue growth and expanding enterprise client adoption.

data.title: Boeing Stock Slides -- China Hits U.S. Defense Firms with Sanctions
data.description:
Boeing stock slid about 1% on Friday morning after China imposed sanctions on 20 U.S. defense companies and 10 executives in response to Washington's recent approval of a large arms sale to Taiwan.

The sanctions freeze any assets the firms hold in China and bar future business dealings with Chinese entities, Beijing's foreign ministry said.

Among the firms targeted are Boeing's defense operations, Northrop Grumman and L3Harris Maritime Services. Executives on the list include the founder of defense tech firm Anduril Industries. Analysts view the measures as largely symbolic, given that most of the companies do little business in China and many already face trade restrictions.

The sanctions follow the United States' announcement of an approximately $11.1 billion weapons package for Taiwan, one of the largest in U.S. history, which Beijing strongly condemned as violating its sovereignty claims over the island.

China's foreign ministry reiterated that Taiwan remains at the core of its national interests and that actions perceived to support Taipei militarily will prompt countermeasures.

Shares of several affected defense names showed muted moves after the announcement, reflecting investor belief that the restrictions will have limited impact on operating results.

data.title: Warner Bros. Discovery Shares Drop as Paramount Skydance Threatens Legal Action
data.description:
Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD, Financials) shares declined after reports that Paramount Skydance could abandon its $30 per share all-cash takeover proposal and consider legal action over how the company's board handled the negotiations.

According to the New York Post, Paramount Skydance executives accused Warner Bros. Discovery's leadership of ignoring their sixth all-cash offer while favoring a competing proposal from Netflix that combined cash and stock. The report also cited concerns over a potential conflict of interest tied to the long-standing relationship between Warner Bros. Discovery CEO David Zaslav and Netflix chief Ted Sarandos.

The potential lawsuit would challenge the fairness of the bidding process and add uncertainty to Warner Bros. Discovery's ongoing strategic review. The company has not commented publicly on the report.

Investors acted immediately as shares plummeted over 2% premarket. Analysts said the dispute might make merger talks tougher and lower investor confidence as the media business consolidates.

data.title: Biohaven's Epilepsy Opportunity Seen Outshining Depression Trial — Market Talk
data.description: Biohaven still has strong potential despite a Phase 2 study of BHV-7000 not meeting its primary endpoint to treat depression, Raymond James analysts say. The analysts continue to rate the stock as a strong buy because they are focused on Biohaven's plans to develop BHV-7000 to treat epilepsy. BHV-7000 has a lot of promise as an epilepsy treatment, including a potentially best-in-class safety profile compared with competitor azetukalner, the analysts say. Biohaven plans to share data in January and have its first pivotal readout for epilepsy in the first half of 2026, which the analysts think will boost its near-term value. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

data.title: S&P 500's post-Election Day run nears 20%
data.description:
The S&P 500's post-election day rally has been incredible, to say the very least, but still falls short of the 2009 period. The benchmark index has surged 19.9% since the market closed on November 5, 2024, according to data from Dow Jones Market Data.

MarketWatch puts the S&P 500 on pace for its lowest gain from Election Day through the end of the following year since 2008.

That was when a 10.9% rise followed Barack Obama's first victory in the same window. The historical comparison matters as the 200809 stretch came as investors clawed back from the financial crisis.2025's jump, though, was built on a sturdier mix of cooling inflation, shifting rate expectations, and robust enthusiasm for AI-powered earnings.

Even with the S&P 500 hovering near record territory late in 2025, the more modest two-year climb points to how choppy the path can be when valuations stay elevated.

data.title: S&P 500 Hits Record High Ahead of 2026
data.description:
U.S. equity benchmarks rose on Friday, with the S&P 500 climbing to a fresh all?time high as traders returned from the Christmas holiday.

The broad S&P 500 index was up about 0.2% in morning trading, putting the benchmark on track for weekly gains of more than 1%. The Nasdaq Composite also edged higher, while the Dow Jones Industrial Average was slightly lower.

For the week, all three major indexes have advanced, reflecting resilient buying interest after a series of holiday closures.

Traders noted that the market is entering a historically strong seasonal period, with the so?called Santa Claus rally, a stretch between the final trading days of the year and early January, often associated with modest gains.

Investors also cited upbeat data from China showing rebounds in key economic activity as another mild positive.

Precious metals extended recent strength, with gold and silver prices reaching new record levels in thin trading conditions.

Market participants are looking ahead to earnings and economic data in early 2026 for further direction.

data.title: Apple Stock Rises Ahead of 2026. What's Driving the Growth
data.description:
Apple shares edged higher about 0.5% on Friday morning after fresh China smartphone shipment figures and a U.S. court decision boosted sentiment in thin holiday trading.

Data from the China Academy of Information and Communications Technology showed that shipments of foreign?branded mobile phones, including Apple's iPhone models, climbed about 128% in November from a year earlier, while total phone shipments in China rose modestly in the same period, Reuters calculations showed. This marked a rebound in demand for foreign brands in the world's largest smartphone market.

In a separate development, a U.S. district court denied Masimo's request to block Apple Watch imports as part of a patent dispute, allowing redesigned smartwatches to continue entering the U.S. market.

The Masimo case stems from earlier litigation in which a jury awarded roughly $634 million for patent infringement related to Apple Watch health features; Apple is appealing that verdict.

Investors said the mix of stronger China demand data and the temporary legal outcome helped lift sentiment for Apple shares.

Analysts noted that sustained gains in China iPhone shipments and clarity on the legal front could influence Apple's performance in early 2026 as smartphone trends evolve.

data.title: Waymo Expands Robotaxi Footprint as 2026 Becomes a Crucial Test
data.description:
Bloomberg reporting suggests Waymo's accelerating expansion could mark an important phase in testing whether autonomous ride-hailing can develop into a viable, potentially profitable business at scale. After operating robotaxis in cities such as San Francisco and Los Angeles, Waymo has said it is targeting more than 1 million rides per week by the end of next year, a level it has already reached on a monthly basis since the spring. Bloomberg News has also reported that Waymo is in talks to raise funds at a valuation near $100 billion, which would roughly double its valuation from a year earlier, alongside an annual revenue run rate above $350 million. As of recent public disclosures, Waymo is permitted to or testing its technology in 26 of the top 30 US metro areas and plans to launch in 12 additional cities in 2026, including London, with early deployments expected to remain limited in size.

The expansion reflects a partnership-led approach that could help manage costs and operational complexity as the business scales. Waymo expanded into Austin and Atlanta in 2025 with Uber , bringing its commercial presence to five cities, and has worked with Uber-backed fleet managers on tasks such as cleaning, charging, and maintenance. Future launches are expected to introduce additional partners, including Lyft in Nashville and Avis Budget Group in Dallas. Gaining exposure to multiple geographies, regulatory regimes, and operating models could give Waymo useful insights as it competes with rivals such as Tesla and Amazon's Zoox, which have also introduced robotaxi services at a smaller scale. Alphabet Chief Executive Officer Sundar Pichai has said internally that the business is not expected to be financially meaningful to the parent company until 202728.

As Waymo has scaled, Bloomberg reporting indicates the company has encountered operational and technical challenges that highlight remaining uncertainties. These have included so-called edge cases such as interactions with stopped school buses, police activity, and incorrect turns, as well as a temporary suspension of service during a major San Francisco power outage when vehicles became immobilized. Waymo has said its data show significantly fewer pedestrian injury crashes than human drivers and that safety remains central to its strategy. Other developers are also preparing customer-facing launches in 2026, with Lucid and Nuro planning to offer self-driving vehicles for Uber riders in San Francisco and additional global markets. With multiple deployments expected next year, 2026 could provide clearer signals on whether robotaxi technology can translate into sustainable earnings over time.

data.title: XRP Positions for 10% Price Move: Details
data.description:
XRP has suspended its recent correction after moving back into the green territory during its previous trading session. As such, the asset is showing signs of consolidation, as its price action continues to form a defined triangle pattern.

According to data provided by popular crypto analyst Ali Martinez, XRP is trading between converging trendlines, which indicates a balance between short-term buyers and sellers as volatility contracts. This implies that a major price move for XRP may be approaching.

Ali Charts
@alicharts

$XRP is consolidating in a triangle, setting up a potential 10% move. pic.twitter.com/qmBjSVmidj

Dec 26, 2025

XRP eyes major rally

After a few days of mild price correction, XRP has resumed its rally, and it is now trading at $1.8684, following a 2.01% price surge over the last 24 hours.

Following its recent bounce, XRP has been holding above near-term support levels, with its price currently stabilizing near the middle of the triangle structure.

While XRP has continued to show mixed price action over the past months, its near-term performance has shown modest strength. 

As such, the token is up 1.97% over the last day and 3.35% over the past week, signaling a mild recovery after recent selling pressure. 

Nonetheless, it is important to note that XRP’s mid-term performance remains weak, as it has recorded a notable decline of 15.17% over the past month and 14.58% over the last six months. Overall, XRP has lost all its 2025 gains, showing a 10.20% year-to-date decline.

XRP prepares to rally 10%

Notably, the analyst shared a chart showing that XRP is forming a triangle, a pattern that often precedes sharp directional moves once price breaks above resistance or below support.

If the breakout becomes successful, the analyst predicts that the asset could move rapidly, recording an increase of up to about 10%, attributed to growing demand and a potential volume surge.

While the breakout would require sustained buying pressure for XRP, it is important to note that a drop below support could halt the impending rally, causing the asset to dip sharply.

data.title: Herbalife stock price rebounded in 2025: will the rally continue?
data.description:
The Herbalife stock price staged a strong bullish breakout this year, moving from a low of $5 in March to the current $14, pushing its market capitalization to over $1.4 billion. So, will the HLF share price continue its strong rebound in the coming year?

Herbalife stock price gains steam amid its resilient growth 

Herbalife is a top company in the health and wellness industries, focusing mostly on direct selling in the United States and other countries.

The company’s business, which has always been highly controversial, has done relatively well in the past few months as demand for its products has continued rising.

Its most recent results showed that its North American business returned to growth for the first time since the second quarter of 2021, a sign of resilient demand. It also jumped as the number of distributors in its network jumped in most of its regions.

The crucial Latin American business continued to thrive, with its sales rising by 11% to $229 million. Its EMEA and Asia Pacific businesses grew by 2% and 3%, respectively.

The revenue rose by 2.7% in the quarter to $1.3 billion, while the closely-watched earnings before interest, tax, depreciation, and amortization rose to $163 million.

Meanwhile, the company continued to improve its balance sheet by repaying the $147 million in outstanding notes and then reduced the total leverage ratio to 2.8x.

Analysts are optimistic about the company’s turnaround

Wall Street analysts are optimistic that the company’s revenue growth will continue in the coming weeks. The average estimate among the three analysts tracking the company is that its revenue will be $1.25 billion this quarter, up by 3.38% from what it made last year.

Its annual revenue estimate is a modest 0.20% increase to $5 billion, followed by $5.12 billion in the coming year. They also expect that its earnings-per-share will move to $2.15 this year and $2.75 in the coming year.

There are also signs that the company has become highly undervalued as its forward price-to-earnings ratio has moved to 7.6, much lower than the sector median of 18.8 and its five-year average of 9.

Similarly, the company’s forward EV-to-EBITDA multiple of 5.43 is also much lower than the sector median of 10.3, also much lower than its historical averages. 

The Herbalife stock price has also jumped as investors bet on its turnaround strategy, which includes more product launches, digital transformation, including the Pro2col app rollout to nearly 8,000 distributors, margin optimization, and debt reduction.

Herbalife share price technical analysis

The weekly chart shows that the HLF stock price has rebounded in the past few months as the company’s turnaround continued. It has already moved above the 50-week and 100-week Exponential Moving Averages (EMA).

The stock is also nearing the 23.6% Fibonacci Retracement level at $18. It also formed an inverse head-and-shoulders pattern, while the Relative Strength Index (RSI) and the Stochastic Oscillator have all pointed upwards.

Therefore, the most likely scenario is where the HLF stock will continue rising as bulls target the 23.6% retracement level at $18. This price target is about 25% above the current level.

data.title: China Sanctions U.S. Defense Firms
data.description:
China is sending another clear message on Taiwan, announcing sanctions against U.S. defense companies days after Washington approved a fresh round of arms sales to the island.

Beijing said it will target 20 U.S. defense-related firms and 10 executives following more than $11 billion in U.S. weapons sales to Taiwan. The list includes Northrop Grumman, L3Harris Technologies, and Boeing's St. Louis operations. The sanctions freeze any assets the companies or individuals hold in China and bar Chinese businesses from dealing with them.

Several executives, including the founder of Anduril Industries, were also banned from entering the country. In practical terms, the fallout is expected to be limited, as most of the firms have little exposure to China and some were already restricted.

Still, Beijing's tone was firm. The foreign ministry called Taiwan the first red line in U.S.-China relations. even symbolic sanctions highlight how sensitive Taiwan remains and why defense names often sit at the center of geopolitical tensions.

data.title: Nvidia's Groq Deal Provides Limited Technological Benefit — Market Talk
data.description: Nvidia's licensing agreement with Groq is a confusing move and the benefits for Nvidia appear limited, says D.A. Davidson analyst Alex Platt. Groq's chips don't have much of a technological advantage in today's market and are useful only for a limited set of workloads, not for frontier AI models." The reality is, we have a hard time seeing this as a deal for technology reasons," Platt says. "These specialized architectures can be excellent in narrow slices of inference, but they don't generalize well to the kind of workloads the frontier is converging on." (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)
